speaker: interviewee
text: Alex, you must stay here, because the lady's going to take--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's Tuesday, April the 30th, 1996. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Ludwig Weiler. This is tape five. Due to circumstances, the family are here at the moment, so we're going to do this at this point. So, Mr. Weiler, would you like to introduce your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've already spoken to the tape, so I don't need any introduction. My wife is Paula, maiden name [? Norman. ?] Originally born in Vienna in late December, 1936. As a baby, her parents and grandparents escaped from Austria, from Vienna, to Belgium, from where, in due course, they managed to go to Australia just before the outbreak of the war. And I got to know her in 1953, two years after I went to Australia. And we got married in 1955. And my first child, or our first child, is Monica, who is standing behind me. And she was born in January, 1960. She's married to the gentleman standing next to her by name of Rony Rosenthal, who himself was born after the war in Germany. But his parents come also from Hungary. His mother maiden name Wiesel from Maramures, Sighet, which is a well-known name-- relation of Elie Wiesel, who is well-known in these matters. His father, who I didn't know, who died prior to getting to know him, also came from eastern Hungary, [INAUDIBLE]. And their respective children Nicky, whose Jewish name is Simcha [INAUDIBLE], Simcha being my late brother's Jewish name, and [INAUDIBLE] after my son-in-law's father. And the next one is-- he is 15. And next one is Talya. She's quite a capable student, a very capable story-teller, who is going to be 10 in November. And then the younger one, who is Daniela, who is a funny one and who was six in January. So she's in her seventh year. There I go on to my son, whose name is Irwin. That is in the English name, named after my late brother. And in Jewish name, he has got my father's name, Shlomo, and [INAUDIBLE] after my late father-in-law. And [INAUDIBLE], which basically it was a rabbinical advice which I received that usually you don't name children after people who deceased in unusual and early age. My parents had unfortunately got killed in an air crash in 1957 being on a business trip. So an older person's name who died in normal circumstances being my wife's maternal grandfather, who died in Austria in 1937, was added. And so he has got three Jewish names. His wife is Dalia, nee Franco. Her grandfather, who was also through the war in various stages, is a very capable man, and who has helped to assist to escape to a large number of his family. That would be a story for him to tell. Her parents are-- obviously, the son was born after the war. And his mother, whose maiden name was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Zauderer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Zauderer. The Zauderer family also originate from Vienna. Mr. [INAUDIBLE], her grandfather, as a young man escaped from Vienna and has got his own story to tell. And then their respective children. The older one, the girl, whose name is Riki. She's a good schoolgirl who is going to be five in June.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a [? monster. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the youngest one, whose name is Alex. He is two years old and is making a nuisance of himself. And that is more or less the total story I want to tell about them, unless they wish to say anything for themselves. I invite my son-in-law, if he wants to say anything, being the oldest of the group, after us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I'm going to pass over to my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't have too much to say. But I'd just like to say I'm really proud of the way my father has conducted his life and brought up our family. And I've grown up with a tremendous sense of security, even though I'm a child of a victim of the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, Irwin, you want to say anything, being the other end of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose the interesting point was when I was in yeshiva in Israel many years ago, I once went along to a memorial for the town which my father came from. And I think the statistics are there were 2,000 people in his town, of which 200 survived. And when I went along-- I'd never met any of the people there-- one of them asked me who I was, so I explained who my father and family were. So he says, anybody who survived the war, it was a miracle. But, he said, for someone to have survived at my father's age was a unique miracle at that age. And it is interesting in that, amongst my contemporaries-- even you can see with my wife-- I'm probably the youngest of the generation born still to have parents who have survived the war. And, in many respects, one therefore sees the differences between my outlook on life and even my wife or contemporaries, because of the difference of who our parents were and what they went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yep. Anyone else like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Talya? Nicky?

speaker: interviewer
text: Paula, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: For me, it's a great pleasure to be here together with my family. My husband must thoroughly enjoy this, because it is a family after he lost everybody. So it's a statement in itself. And we're happy to be here all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now Alex, can you stop it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyone else? This is your opportunity.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to say something, Riki?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dalia? Any words of wisdom? Talya? What--

speaker: interviewer
text: Talya said she'd like to say something, but she wouldn't know what to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I'll say something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead, Nicky.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my grandfather in December to Yad Vashem. And it was very nice to hear from a first-hand person. Like, he told me, like, completely, like, other people might think it's not really the truth and other things. And he really puts it how it really was and where it came from, and what these type of countries were like to-- and explained about all the concentration camps and all the different things about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose if we had to add something on behalf of the next generation, in many respects, when you have a war, there's always a question of who wins the war and who loses. And I think, in that respect, the fact that my father was able to bring up a traditional Jewish family with the same traditions, very much in the same ways his family had been brought up, and, of course, been able to secure us a decent living, is in itself the proof that we've won, and we've survived in a very positive manner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I do feel, too, that today, when I'm faced with any anti-Semitism, that I feel, due to the fact that my father is here and brought up a family, I feel very much today it's their problem, and I do not take the problem upon myself. And if they don't like it, it's their tough.

speaker: interviewee
text: Short to the point.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trying to maintain a chronological order, I think the main thrust of my story stopped at 1939. And excuse me if I transgressed into political matters and otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I would like to say that it was in 1940 when there was the last year that the Hungarian Jews were drafted in the Hungarian army. And that was the time when the Hungarians, unfortunately for all the Jews, took over the Romanian Transylvania, the Romanian-- Hungarian part of Romania. Of course, according to the Treaty of Vienna of 1940. And I remember my uncle being, when I went to Sopron to my grandparents, all dressed up in army uniform. He was a driver from an ambulance, and went.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what I really would like to come back in that, after that time, the Hungarian fascist, anti-Jewish government decided to create work labor forces, which was called [HUNGARIAN] in Hungarian. It means work camp. For all Jews of military age, which was basically for 18 till, I think, 48 or 50. They extended it afterwards by a couple of years. These groups were mainly regimented and controlled by a rather illiterate and cruel or crude Hungarian individuals who were called [HUNGARIAN]. That's a name of basically the supervisor, or the person in charge. These were all of lower rank. There were a few camps where the officer was more of a gentleman but, on the whole, that was the beginning of the active persecution of the military-age Jews. Now, as soon as the-- 1941, the Russian war started, because this is really when the Hungarians got involved in a war-like situation. These people were taken out in the main to the Ukraine, which the Germans very quickly occupied after the campaign in the summer of 1941, and were given the most dangerous tasks without any training whatsoever, such as mine sweeping. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Practically like human shields, not that they were shields. And they were subjected to the most atrocious cruelties and treatment. And it must be understood by people that dying is easy. It's till you get there. And the mental anguish, the physical starvation or mistreatment, is really the bad part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what I'm saying is, there was a saying in Birkenau and in the camps generally. The lucky ones were the ones who got killed right away, because at least they didn't suffer very long. The reason I mention it is because I had a young, Jewish boy-- he was from the [INAUDIBLE] community-- who was an extremely intelligent boy. His name was Laci Stossl. And he-- his parents were not poor, but not so comfortable. So as a senior student, he took other students to give them extra lessons in certain subject which the parents wanted them to do better. And I had a few lessons with this Laci Stossl. And he was older than my brother, and he was taken into one of these camps who were taken to Ukraine. And I remember the horrifying story which came back that he was literally tied to the-- in the deepest, coldest Russian winter in front of an engine, of a railway engine, and he froze to death on the engine.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the reason-- I mean, I could tell you many of these incidents. The reason-- and these were-- this account was given by eyewitnesses who were in the same company, but were not-- happened to be lucky not to have been chosen to do that. The reason I'm trying to tell you is that the atrocities, they were the worst part of it. When it came to somebody dying, either it happened in a minute or it happened in three days. That was the end. But till you get there. And you see, a lot of them tried to escape in the front line to the Russians. And, unfortunately, our Russian friends were not quite as magnanimous to the Jews as they should have been. And many, many, many perished in Russian hands. Maybe not particularly being mistreated. Even so, there was a very high degree of very serious anti-Jewish sentiment in the Russian army. And the reason I mention this because my late uncle, the Dr. Weiler who lived in Budapest, his brother-in-law, who was liberated in Budapest when the Russian army came in, was hiding in a cellar or bunker with a number of other people. And I knew the man after the war. When the first Russian soldiers came in, they started shouting, [RUSSIAN], which means, in Russian, Jewish. And one of the soldiers took out the machine gun and he shot off his lower jaw. In other words, there weren't many Jews in the army. And if they happened to fall in their hands, they were lucky. There were decent ones who didn't care, but there were a lot of serious anti-Semites amongst them. I mean, I don't have to go into world history to tell what Russian pogroms in anti-Semitism was about. But so much so that I remember, after the war, the Russians captured the Vlasov guard. The-- General Vlasov was a Ukrainian who created a military battalion, which he commanded, of Ukrainians who worked alongside the SS. Now, these people, who were called the Black SS, they were the worst criminals. Actually, the same as the Germans used the Jews to do their organization of deportation or organization in the camps, they also knew that they used the worst, cruelest, totally inhumane, barbaric people to do their dirty job. Once they were Germans, no question about it. A lot of SS belonged to that category. Nevertheless, the most serious atrocities were committed by the auxiliary SS brigades, like the Lithuanian groups and the Ukrainians. They were known to be the most ruthless killers. And they were the ones who they set on the partisans in the Ukraine. Anyway, after the war, when the Russians caught them, they brought them into Sopron. And I watched them being led along the route. They actually forbid anybody to open the windows, and had to draw the curtains. I mean, obviously if there were not curtains, it didn't matter. And they had to go head down, bowed. And they took them out to a very old prison which was nearby, the town called [INAUDIBLE]. And from there, they were shipped back to Russia. And, I guarantee you, Stalin didn't leave one of them alive. Because in fact, he liquidated a lot of the Russian soldiers who came back because they saw too much. Anyway. Again, I'm transgressing from the Jewish subject. But this is important to remember that the Ukrainian community, especially in the United States, complained that Jews accused them, whether it's them or another one. And it doesn't matter. Believe you me, any Ukrainian I've met, I've not yet met one who was in the slightest manner sympathetic to a Jew or Jewish suffering. And that is putting it mildly, and I think most people who have been in the camps can bear this out. I don't know whether I mentioned it, but it should be remembered that, in the middle of '42, '43 period, in the German army, 16% of the army consisted of non-Germans. These were all the ethnic Germans, Ukrainians, Lithuanians. And these were the murderers. These really carried out-- as I mentioned to you about Catholic anti-Semitism, the same applies. Whilst the north Germans, the Prussians, were militaristic people and didn't like the Jews, they were not outright child killers. They may not have minded if Jews are killed, but they didn't stoop to that level. Like the Wehrmacht, who were generally quite OK, you know. I can't say the same about the Austrians or the Hungarians or the Slovaks or so on. But the Germans as such, you know, it only reminds me that what happened in Germany was the same as in any other country. 2%, 3% of the scum came on top of the water, and they frightened the rest of them. It's no excuse that-- what they did. And I'm not excusing them generally that they didn't willingly participate in expropriating Jewish properties and causing all the anguish and harm to the people till the end they got killed in Poland and in the camps. But, nevertheless, the cruelties and the real atrocities, wherever you see a war crime-- details being written or explained-- were mostly from this group of people. And I think-- I'm not trying to excuse the Germans. By no means. I have no brief to act as their defender. But I think it should be put in the right perspective.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about what your life was like in the period when the war was started but hadn't affected Hungary directly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Interesting. The situation in our town, as I explained already, was relatively calm and uneventful. There were occasional little skirmishes where a Jew may have been hit or being spat at or being abused, and he just walked the other way because there was no point to put up a fight. Nevertheless, I cannot seriously suggest that we were physically intimidated on a regular basis. We lived an absolutely normal life in the sense and in the atmosphere of the serious anti-Semitism which has been around us. I'm purely talking physically. I could get dressed normally. On Shabbat, I was dressed like on Shabbat. I went to school. I went back from school. I walked home with my non-Jewish school friends. Insofar as the normal activities were concerned, we were basically not seriously affected. There you can already take the other side of the coin. There were a lot of businesspeople whose livelihoods have been ruinated. There were people who couldn't go to attend to proper schools, couldn't do their studies. So all this has happened. But I'm trying to separate the basic restrictions on one's freedom from actual physical harm. And I'm really referring here to the actual physical harm when I'm saying that we lived a normal life. When I call not normal is when you get your window broken every minute or you can't safely walk home. And, in that respect, I must say that till March 1944, life was relatively normal. For instance, schooling, we had to use one school, the lyceum, where the gymnasium went over. And we had, in turn, afternoon lessons when else the lyceum morning lesson because, out of the biggest school, our school, they made a military hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were probably shortages of food. There was rationing. We have not suffered under that. My father had been in the flour mill business. We had ample food. In fact, I must say an interesting anecdote-- and now I'm jumping to the day before the deportation.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarian Gendarmerie, who were ultra-cruel people, worse than the SS practically, I want to say, literally took every head of family where they knew that they were reasonably well-to-do-- which most of the time were-- into an interrogation in the Jewish Talmud Torah, which consisted of absolute torture and physical [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] attacks on people to get out of them where they hid their valuables. Gold, et cetera. Money, whatever. And my father was also taken. And in a miraculous manner-- you can imagine, if you are beaten half-dead a day before you get deported to Auschwitz, what chance you had at the selection. Zero. So he was also taken. And it was his habit-- and, I'm sorry to say, my habit-- to have large volume of food. He was a very large man. He bought the things in quantities which we didn't use, which we had in stock. Whether it was soap or whether it was flour or oil or egg, our house was known to be the most well-stocked house in town. And he hid, or he had in one of our stores, which was also one of our properties, a bag of poppy seeds, a few bags of flour, some goose fat, and some other minor food. And when it came to the interrogation, they asked him, where did you put your money. We're going to hit it out from you, and you're not leaving here alive unless you hand it over. He said, look, I'm a merchant and an agent of my father-in-law who had the flour mill in [INAUDIBLE]. And I have got no jewelry. But what I can tell you is I've got this and this food in this and this store. They were so delighted to put their hands on-- when in 1944 there was already great shortages of sugar and everything-- that they let him go. They didn't touch him. And it was a miracle, you know. Because in spite of that, they could have done it. But I'm trying to say that people think money's most valuable. It isn't. Sometimes food or water is more valuable-- not sometimes, all times-- than money. Money is very helpful. It helps to grease the wheels of industry. But it is not the end. Now, coming back off my-- so, therefore, I can report a relatively normal existence in a wartime situation where we were a somewhat oppressed minority, belabored under anti-Nuremberg legislations, but not having the daily events of Berlin, Vienna, or Krakow, if you know what it means. These demonstrations and things, yes, they had sometimes demonstrations. The [NON-ENGLISH], this was this youth movement. And they-- every autumn-time they had a sort of a procession. And then it was better to stay indoors and not to be seen on the street, because they were being instigated by the leadership to do some harm. I mean, you can see when I mentioned before that they put this chemical on Jewish shop windows, and they made life difficult. So I'm not saying that nothing happened, but nothing serious to the point of anybody being killed or maimed beyond getting well afterwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this, I'm afraid, is maybe a negative, but it's a true statement. There's no point to exaggerate which didn't happen. Enough happened anyway, so we don't have to add. We should rather take away, so there should be always something extra to add in case somebody has any doubt about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now if I just may refer to my notes, because I'm losing-- yes. So I left off in 1940. There were-- one of the anti-Jewish legislations were that Jews were forbidden the slaughter of animals, except for chicken and turkey and goose. So we had no meat supply. That was stopped altogether. It didn't exist after 1940. But we had enough other food it was not a problem. For instance, minor things. For instance, kosher milk was difficult to get, those who really insisted on. And I remember these partners of my father. They had a huge house on the main square going onto another square [INAUDIBLE]. And they were the only people I knew who had cows in town, because they had such an estate there practically. And being my father's partners, I used to go there twice a week in the afternoon after school when there was milking time. Because you know, kosher milking only consists that you have to watch it while somebody's doing it. And then we got our quantity, whatever we wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was no problem. But if you don't have kosher milk, you can also exist. That's not the last word.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what I'm trying to indicate that our life was normal. We had our domestic, non-Jewish help. We held onto the men. Whereas in many businesses where they tried to Aryanize it-- in other words, take it away from Jews, like factories and big industries in Budapest who Jews had-- they had a problem, because there they moved in people, in fact, to take it away, or to put in already non-Jews in command what goes on, and so on. Now, my father, being in the real estate business, this didn't occur. His side line was the supply to the-- which was a military center shop. And he was selling to the army grain and flour and so on until 1943. And they didn't know that he was a Jew.
